content.message:
"Software engineering hiring is back!" Technically true. Also kind of misleading.

The Pragmatic Engineer's 2026 jobs market deepdive (data from TrueUp and Workforce.ai) found that top tech companies are hiring 20% more engineers than last year, and Google's engineering postings are up 62% year-over-year. Good news, right?

Look closer and the growth is lumpy. Ramp grew engineering headcount 94% in two years. Wiz grew 84%. Datadog grew 68%. Fintech, security, and observability are absorbing most of the new demand. Meanwhile Meta laid off 10% of staff last week, Oracle is cutting up to 30,000 roles, and entry-level postings are still 28% below their 2022 peak.

Aggregate hiring numbers make a great headline but a bad personal career strategy. The real question isn't "is the market up" — it's "is my specific lane one of the ones growing."

Source: The Pragmatic Engineer, "State of the software engineering job market in 2026" (newsletter.pragmaticengineer.com/p/state-of-the-job-market-2026)

What's your experience been? Been sitting with this. What do you think?

content.caption:
"The job market is back!" — sure, if you're reading one headline.

Swipe through — the data tells a messier story than the stat everyone's quoting.

The Pragmatic Engineer's 2026 jobs deepdive (TrueUp + Workforce.ai data) shows real growth, but it's lumpy: fintech, security, and observability are absorbing it. Entry-level and several Big Tech orgs are going the other way.

The market isn't one market. It's a sorting mechanism — and which lane you're in matters more than the aggregate number on the headline.

Am I wrong? Tell me. Save this one.

(Source in comments 👇)

#Software #AI #DevSecOps #FutureOfWork #TechJobs #CareerGrowth
content.first_comment: Source: The Pragmatic Engineer, "State of the software engineering job market in 2026" (May 26, 2026) — data via TrueUp and Workforce.ai. https://newsletter.pragmaticengineer.com/p/state-of-the-job-market-2026
